meta.title: Narrative Text Stress — Frame Titles, Taglines, Big-Idea Statements, Recap Bullets, And Long Prose In Every Slot
meta.subject: the engine's text-fitting contract for prose-shaped scenes

scenes.title: A Frame Title Long Enough To Force The Renderer's Tiered Auto-Shrink Down Three Steps Before The Word "Renderer" Reaches The Right Edge Of The Safe Band
scenes.tagline: The taglines a real explainer carries are often longer than the slide-ware designer's original Lorem Ipsum — a sentence that names the misconception the film must kill, plus a clause that names what survives the kill. This fixture's tagline is exactly that shape, two hundred and ninety characters long, and the renderer wraps it to four lines at a comfortable size rather than letting it overrun the column or bleed past the title above.
scenes.footnote: Compared with the gallery films, this fixture trades coherence for the worst-case length distribution; each scene asks its renderer one question — does the long-text strategy hold? — and the answer the eye can read off the rendered video is the contract.

beats.narration: This fixture is the narrative torture test — frame titles, taglines, big-idea statements, recap bullets, every prose-shaped slot pushed past its expected length.
The strategy is uniform: wrap to a documented line count first, shrink before the floor, ellipsis after — never a mid-word clip.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // PASSAGE — A SHORT PRIMARY SOURCE WITH ANNOTATIONS THAT ARE LONG SENTENCES, NOT TWO-WORD POSTILS
scenes.heading: passage scene — the artifact is short, the marks are full sentences, and the annotation column has to host them inside its width
scenes.text:
Whose woods these are I think I know.
His house is in the village though;
He will not see me stopping here
To watch his woods fill up with snow.

marks.quote: His house is in the village though
marks.note: A claim about location that is also a claim about ownership and absence — the speaker is in the woods, the owner is in the village, and the woods are the disputed middle ground the poem will walk through.
The image the stanza closes on is the one the rest of the poem will return to — accumulation, weight, the slow filling of a container — and we will see it again when the speaker thinks about sleep.

beats.narration: The two marks each carry an annotation that is a full sentence — far longer than a typical postil. The annotation column wraps and shrinks rather than truncating mid-thought.
On the second mark the eye lands on the image the poem will return to.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // CLOSEUP — FILE PATH LONG ENOUGH TO TEST THE WINDOW CHROME AND A FULL-SENTENCE BEAT NOTE
scenes.heading: closeup scene — the file path stretches the window chrome and the per-beat note carries a full claim, not a fragment

beats.narration: The file path is long enough that the window chrome has to shrink it; the code body shows the API the rest of the fixture has been calling all the way through.
beats.note: The annotation note pinned beneath the window is also a full sentence — it should auto-shrink, wrap to a couple of lines, and never bleed past the window's bottom edge into the wordmark band.

beats.narration: The full shape of the props — the contract the helper exposes — is a slot description, not a free-form configuration.
beats.note: Mode is the strategy chooser; maxLines bounds the wrap; floorPx bounds the shrink. Together they make the failure mode predictable, which is the property the depth-review eye needs to confirm.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // TENSION — THE TRADE-OFF THE NARRATIVE FIXTURE HAS TO NAME
scenes.heading: tension scene — what the long-text strategy gives up in exchange for what it guarantees

nodes.label: A documented floor and a bounded line count over a clean ellipsis fallback
nodes.sub: We chose the predictable failure mode — every slot's worst case is documented, and a depth-review eye can audit it at a glance without running anything.

nodes.label: Optimizing for the typical case with a generous floor and no documented ceiling
nodes.sub: Rejected — the worst case becomes invisible: a short label looks fine, but a long label in a film a year from now blows past the column without any signal.

nodes.label: The readability floor is documented per-slot but not validated end-to-end against narration speed
nodes.sub: A 12-px floor that reads cleanly at 1920×1080 may not read at 1280×720, and the contract does not yet bind floors to render scale.

beats.narration: We chose the predictable failure mode over the prettier typical case. The bill is that a corner case never looks great — it shrinks visibly or truncates with an ellipsis — instead of working until it doesn't.

The residual risk is that the floor is hand-tuned per slot rather than validated against narration speed. A read-it-once-and-move-on cadence might tolerate a different floor than a hold-and-savour one.

scenes.heading: big-idea scene — one sentence the viewer should leave with
scenes.statement: Bound the slot, document the floor, fail predictably.

beats.narration: The big-idea contract is exactly twenty words or fewer — the renderer's auto-shrink covers anything longer that ever slips through.

scenes.heading: recap scene — bullets long enough that the wrap-and-shrink path is the one the renderer takes for each
points: Every prose-shaped slot in the engine — frame titles, taglines, footnotes, passage annotations, closeup notes, big-idea statements, recap bullets — has been pushed past its base size; each one wraps to its bounded line count or auto-shrinks past the wrap budget without any mid-word clipping or off-frame bleed.

What the fixture proves is the predictability of the failure mode, not that every slot is happy at every length. A 300-character recap bullet still looks like a 300-character bullet; it just behaves like the contract said it would, with the U-plus-2026 ellipsis at the end of the trailing line where the line cap kicks in.

The trade-off the engine makes is uniformly visible: a depth-review eye watching this fixture once can audit every long-text strategy without leaving the rendered video — and that is the property the contract was written to give.

beats.narration: Three long bullets — each wraps and shrinks under the contract; the rejected alternative would have been 4 short bullets, which would have hidden the test from the depth-review eye. The contract's main limit is real: a tight 12-px floor cannot rescue a slot whose surrounding geometry is itself broken.
